categoryName: Pharmacokinetics: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics
questions.stem: This plasma drug concentration versus time plot is a straight, linearly declining line. What elimination kinetics does this pattern represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: First-order elimination

choices.label: B
choices.text: Zero-order elimination

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enterohepatic recirculation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Flow-limited elimination

choices.label: E
choices.text: Concentration-independent absorption

explanation.summary: A constant absolute amount of drug removed per unit time, independent of concentration, yields a straight line on a linear plot, characteristic of zero-order kinetics.
explanation.whyCorrect: A constant absolute amount of drug removed per unit time, independent of concentration, yields a straight line on a linear plot, characteristic of zero-order kinetics.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics
questions.stem: This schematic shows a constant 2.5 units/h of drug removed at every concentration step. Which drug is a classic clinical example of this saturable elimination pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Penicillin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metoprolol

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ethanol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gentamicin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Digoxin

explanation.summary: Ethanol (along with phenytoin and aspirin at high doses) is eliminated at a constant amount per unit time because its metabolic pathway is saturated, defining zero-order kinetics.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ethanol (along with phenytoin and aspirin at high doses) is eliminated at a constant amount per unit time because its metabolic pathway is saturated, defining zero-order kinetics.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics
questions.stem: On a linear y-axis, this plasma concentration versus time curve declines exponentially rather than as a straight line. What does the exponential shape indicate about elimination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An increasing fraction is eliminated as concentration falls

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug is eliminated only above a threshold concentration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elimination stops once concentration reaches half the peak

choices.label: D
choices.text: A constant fraction of drug is eliminated per unit time

choices.label: E
choices.text: A constant absolute amount of drug is eliminated per unit time

explanation.summary: First-order kinetics removes a constant proportion of drug per unit time, so the elimination rate is proportional to concentration and the curve decays exponentially on a linear scale.
explanation.whyCorrect: First-order kinetics removes a constant proportion of drug per unit time, so the elimination rate is proportional to concentration and the curve decays exponentially on a linear scale.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics
questions.stem: In this schematic the elimination rate halves as concentration halves (5 to 2.5 to 1.25 units/h). What quantity remains constant across all concentrations for such a drug?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The half-life

choices.label: B
choices.text: The maximum metabolic elimination capacity

choices.label: C
choices.text: The absolute amount of drug eliminated per hour

choices.label: D
choices.text: The fraction of drug bound to plasma protein

choices.label: E
choices.text: The time required to eliminate the entire dose

explanation.summary: Because a constant proportion is removed per unit time, the time for concentration to fall by half is fixed regardless of the starting concentration, so half-life is constant in first-order kinetics.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because a constant proportion is removed per unit time, the time for concentration to fall by half is fixed regardless of the starting concentration, so half-life is constant in first-order kinetics.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics
questions.stem: This graph compares steady-state acetaminophen concentrations for three formulations. The intravenous curve is nearly flat while the oral curves oscillate between peaks and troughs. What best explains the flat intravenous profile?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous drug bypasses steady state entirely

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous dosing uses a far larger total dose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous drug is not subject to first-order elimination

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuous infusion delivers drug at a rate matching elimination

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous drug has a much shorter half-life

explanation.summary: A continuous IV infusion inputs drug at a constant rate equal to the elimination rate at steady state, so concentration plateaus without the peak-trough swings seen with intermittent oral dosing.
explanation.whyCorrect: A continuous IV infusion inputs drug at a constant rate equal to the elimination rate at steady state, so concentration plateaus without the peak-trough swings seen with intermittent oral dosing.
